title: Bias and money personality
description: Practice understanding of loss aversion, the endowment effect, herd mentality, anchoring, and present bias.

assessment_items.question_content:
Many biases affect how we make financial decisions.

** Which of the following best defines the loss aversion bias?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the preference for immediate rewards over future ones
the tendency to follow the crowd in financial decisions
the tendency to overvalue things that we own
the preference to avoid losses over making gains

assessment_items.question_content:
You have the chance to put money into a new business. The profit could be big but you might also lose a lot. You find yourself worrying too much about the loss, even though the business could make good money. 

**What bias might be influencing your thinking?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

You've just won $\$100$ from a lottery! You know that one of your biases is a loss aversion.

**Based on this, what action are you most likely to take?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: hide it in your desk to keep it safe
buy the same cup that everyone else is buying
keep the winning lottery ticket because you feel it's more valuable now

assessment_items.question_content:
Our values and biases affect many of our financial decisions.

** Which of the following best defines the herd mentality bias?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the preference for immediate rewards over future ones
the tendency to follow the crowd in financial decisions
the tendency to overvalue things that we own
the preference to avoid losses over making gains

assessment_items.question_content:
Everyone in your apartment complex is buying an air purifier. Even though you've never thought of buying one before, you now feel the need to get one too.

**This is an example of which bias?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

You've managed to save $\$500$.

**Knowing that you have a herd mentality bias, what might you do with this money?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: spend it right away on a shopping spree
hide the money in your sock drawer because you fear losing it
buy the same expensive gadget all your friends have been showing off
put the five $\$100$ bills in a frame because you believe they are more valuable now since you own them

assessment_items.question_content:
Biases can often lead to financial mistakes or poor investment decisions.

**Which of the following best describes the endowment effect bias?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: After buying a cup of limited-edition coffee, you believe it tastes much better than regular coffee, even if blind taste tests suggest otherwise.
When a new phone model is released, you decide to buy it right away instead of waiting, even if your current phone is working perfectly fine.
Based on word-of-mouth, you decide to invest in a certain stock without doing thorough research of your own.
Despite the chances of higher returns, you decide to avoid risky investments to keep your savings safe.

assessment_items.question_content:
You've recently bought a rare comic book. Now, even though you've been offered a reasonable price for it, you're unwilling to sell. You feel it's more valuable just because it belongs to you.

**This is an example of which bias?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

You've received an extra $\$100$ as a gift. 

**Considering that your personal bias is endowment effect, what would you do with this money?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Immediately spend it on a concert ticket.
Store it in a safe at home to avoid any possibility of losing it.
Buy the pair of sneakers everyone is wearing
Tuck it away in a special place because you believe it's a special $\$100$ bill.

assessment_items.question_content:
**For each of the following cases, identify whether it's an example of anchoring bias or present bias.**

In a store, the first jacket you see is priced at $\$50$. After that, any jackets priced lower seem like a good deal. [[☃ dropdown 1]]

Having some extra money, you decide to treat yourself and go out for dinner and movies. [[☃ dropdown 2]]

During a job interview, the employer offers you $\$15$ an hour at the beginning. It then becomes difficult for you to ask for your initial goal of $\$18$ per hour. [[☃ dropdown 3]]
hints:
Let's think about what anchoring and present bias mean:

**Anchoring bias** means the first price or number you see influences your thoughts.
**Present bias** means wanting to enjoy something right now, without thinking about the future.

Check each example. 

Ask yourself, is the decision based on the *first info* or the first price they saw? If so, then it is the __anchoring bias__.

Or, are they choosing to have fun *right now* without thinking of the future? If so, then it is the __present bias__.

In a store, the first jacket you see is priced at $\$50$. After that, any jackets priced lower seem like a good deal.  $\boxed{\text{anchoring bias}}$

Having some extra money, you decide to treat yourself and go out for dinner and movies. $\boxed{\text{present bias}}$

During a job interview, the employer offers you $\$15$ an hour at the beginning. It then becomes difficult for you to ask for your initial goal of $\$18$ per hour. $\boxed{\text{anchoring bias}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**For each of the following cases, identify whether it's an example of anchoring bias or present bias.**

You see an advertisement for a phone that's on sale for one day. You buy it right away. [[☃ dropdown 1]]

When searching for apartments, the first one you check costs $\$800$ per month in rent. The price of this first apartment becomes a reference for all other apartments you view. [[☃ dropdown 2]]

Instead of saving your tax refund, you decide to spend it on a tattoo. [[☃ dropdown 3]]
hints:
Let's think about what anchoring and present bias mean:

**Anchoring bias** means the first price or number you see influences your thoughts.
**Present bias** means wanting to enjoy something right now, without thinking about the future.

Check each example. 

Ask yourself, is the decision based on the *first info* or the first price they saw? If so, then it is the __anchoring bias__.

Or, are they choosing to have fun *right now* without thinking of the future? If so, then it is the __present bias__.

You see an advertisement for a phone that's on sale for one day. You buy it right away. $\boxed{\text{present bias}}$

When searching for apartments, the first one you check costs $\$800$ per month in rent. The price of this first apartment becomes a reference for all other apartments you view.$\boxed{\text{anchoring bias}}$

Instead of saving your tax refund, you decide to spend it on a tattoo.$\boxed{\text{present bias}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**For each of the following cases, identify whether it's an example of anchoring bias or present bias.**

When negotiating the price for a used bike, the seller first quotes a price of $\$200$. After some back-and-forth, they lower the price to $\$150$. This now seems like a good deal, even though you initially set out to spend only $\$100$.[[☃ dropdown 1]]

You receive your tax refund and decide to spend it on a weekend getaway immediately. [[☃ dropdown 2]]

While shopping for new car tires, the first price quote you get is $\$900$. After this quote, $\$600$ seems like a fabulous bargain to you, even though these may still be higher than what you had planned to spend. [[☃ dropdown 3]]
hints:
Let's think about what anchoring and present bias mean:

**Anchoring bias** means the first price or number you see influences your thoughts.
**Present bias** means wanting to enjoy something right now, without thinking about the future.

Check each example. 

Ask yourself, is the decision based on the *first info* or the first price they saw? If so, then it is the __anchoring bias__.

Or, are they choosing to have fun *right now* without thinking of the future? If so, then it is the __present bias__.

When negotiating the price for a used bike, the seller first quotes a price of $\$200$. After some back-and-forth, they lower the price to $\$150$. This now seems like a good deal, even though you initially set out to spend only $\$100$.$\boxed{\text{anchoring bias}}$

You receive your tax refund and decide to spend it on a weekend getaway immediately.$\boxed{\text{present bias}}$

While shopping for new car tires, the first price quote you get is $\$900$. After this quote, $\$600$ seems like a fabulous bargain to you, even though these may still be higher than what you had planned to spend.$\boxed{\text{anchoring bias}}$
